metadata.report_title: ATS-proofing for undergraduate résumés (rising seniors) with emphasis on international students in the U.S.
metadata.audience: Rising-senior undergraduates applying to U.S.-based internships and entry-level roles; includes international students (e.g., F-1).
metadata.executive_summary: Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) are recruiting platforms that typically (1) collect candidate data (resume + form fields), (2) parse uploaded résumés into structured fields (e.g., contact info, education, employer, titles), and (3) help recruiters filter, search, and prioritize candidates—often using keyword/skill matching and qualifying (knockout) questions.  The highest-impact ATS-proofing work for students is mostly formatting risk removal (single-column, no tables/text boxes, no images; standard fonts/file types; avoid headers/footers for critical info) and application-level accuracy (review parsed fields; complete every portal field; answer work-authorization/sponsorship questions truthfully and consistently).  International students are disproportionately affected by work-authorization qualifying questions and sponsorship questions; a résumé cannot override an auto-disqualifying answer. Most guidance advises not listing immigration status on the résumé by default, while still being fully truthful in applications and interviews.

effort_reward_criteria.definition: Effort-to-Reward (E:R) reflects how much time/complexity a step typically costs versus how strongly it improves ATS parsing accuracy, reduces auto-filter risk, and increases recruiter discoverability.
effort_dimensions: Time (one-time fix vs. repeated per application; minutes vs. hours)
Complexity (simple formatting change vs. rewriting/quantifying content)
Dependency (needs external info like immigration advising or verification)
reward_dimensions: Searchability (keywords exist in plain text where ATS can index them)
Human readability (recruiters can quickly interpret content)

rating_scale.High: Low-to-moderate effort with large or risk-reducing payoff; do first.
rating_scale.Medium: Moderate effort and/or repeated per application; meaningful payoff when targeted.
rating_scale.Low: High effort or uncertain payoff for most undergrads; consider after High/Medium items.

workflow: Freeze an ATS-safe base template (single column, no tables/graphics, standard fonts) and keep it as your master file.
For each target role, extract requirements/keywords from the job description and map them to the most relevant bullets (experience/projects/skills).
Tailor wording to mirror the job’s exact terminology (include both acronyms and spelled-out forms where relevant).
Run a final ATS check: (a) upload to a resume scanner/optimizer (campus tools like VMock or Big Resume, if available) and (b) ensure the exported file is text-based and under typical parser limits.
Submit through the portal and verify/repair any auto-populated fields before finalizing (especially in Workday-style workflows).
Complete every required application field (don’t rely on résumé upload alone) and double-check qualifying/knockout answers.

If you are an international student, ensure work-authorization and sponsorship answers are truthful and consistent with your situation (typically: authorized now via CPT/OPT; will need future sponsorship).
Log what version you submitted, where, and when (file naming + master versioning) to support iterative improvements.

ats_explanation.how_ats_parsing_works: In many ATS workflows, an uploaded résumé is parsed (converted/scanned into text and mapped to fields) so the system can auto-fill a candidate profile or application fields. For example, Greenhouse describes resume parsing as scanning an imported résumé and auto-filling candidate fields; Workday guides describe populating a professional profile from the résumé; and Lever notes its resume parser can add information such as name, organization, and contact details.
ats_explanation.what_ats_use_the_data_for: Career centers and employers note that ATS help recruiters manage high application volume and often support filtering or prioritization through keywords/skills and other structured criteria. UCI explains ATS scan for keywords, rank candidates, and help narrow pools; NACE similarly notes recruiters use ATS to filter when they cannot read every résumé.
ats_explanation.why_students_get_filtered_even_with_a_go: A common failure mode is not the résumé file itself but the application form: some ATS can automatically filter candidates based on qualifying (knockout) questions. The University of Utah explicitly notes that applicants may be filtered out based on answers to qualifying questions, even though the ATS still scans the résumé mainly to standardize and organize information for recruiters.

common_failure_modes.failure_mode: Critical info placed in headers/footers or text boxes (especially contact info).
common_failure_modes.why_it_fails: Some parsers ignore or misread header/footer regions and text boxes; Greenhouse lists contact info in headers/footers/text boxes as a known cause of parsing issues.
common_failure_modes.international_notes: If your name includes uncommon characters, risk increases when combined with header/footer parsing limitations.

common_failure_modes.failure_mode: Tables/columns/complex layouts that scramble reading order.
common_failure_modes.why_it_fails: ATS typically parse left-to-right, top-to-bottom; columns and tables can scramble fields. UMD warns about reading order; Greenhouse lists columns/tables as common offenders.
common_failure_modes.international_notes: Avoid bilingual side-by-side columns; if you must show multilingual ability, do it in a simple Skills line.

common_failure_modes.failure_mode: Images, graphics, photos, word art, or image-based PDFs.
common_failure_modes.why_it_fails: Some systems cannot parse images; Workday’s guide says image-based files are not supported for resume upload into a Workday profile, and Greenhouse lists graphics/photos as parsing problems.
common_failure_modes.international_notes: Avoid including a photo (also common non-U.S. CV practice) because it commonly adds parsing risk and is discouraged by U.S. career centers.

common_failure_modes.failure_mode: Non-standard file types / excessive file size.
common_failure_modes.why_it_fails: Greenhouse says resumes may fail to import due to being a non-standard file type and cannot be parsed above certain sizes (2.5MB).
common_failure_modes.international_notes: If you submit a résumé exported from a design tool that produces a large, image-heavy PDF, parsing can fail even when upload is allowed.

common_failure_modes.failure_mode: Unrecognized characters or unusual spacing (e.g., letter-spaced words).
common_failure_modes.why_it_fails: Greenhouse notes unrecognized characters can prevent correct import, and it lists 'spaces between letters' as a formatting issue that breaks parsing.
common_failure_modes.international_notes: International names with diacritics may be fine in many systems, but minimizing decorative symbols and unusual characters lowers parser risk.

common_failure_modes.failure_mode: Abbreviations that the parser or recruiter search doesn't normalize reliably (e.g., job titles).
common_failure_modes.why_it_fails: Greenhouse flags incomplete job titles (e.g., 'Sr.' instead of 'Senior') as a parsing issue, and UCI emphasizes exact term matching for keyword scans.
common_failure_modes.international_notes: If you translate titles from non-U.S. contexts, prefer a standard U.S.-equivalent title plus the original in parentheses if needed.

common_failure_modes.failure_mode: Work-authorization and sponsorship screening questions answered incorrectly or inconsistently.
common_failure_modes.why_it_fails: NACE notes the lawful authorization/sponsorship questions and explains most F-1 students will need to answer 'yes' to future sponsorship; Utah notes qualifying questions can filter applicants.
common_failure_modes.international_notes: This is often the single highest-stakes ATS step for international students because it can be a knockout filter.

contact & header.action: Put your contact block in the résumé body (not in Word/PDF headers/footers).
contact & header.description: Top of page 1: full name, phone, email, and optional links—typed as normal body text, not inserted via the header/footer tool.
contact & header.rationale: Header/footer regions and text boxes may not parse reliably; Greenhouse explicitly lists contact info in headers/footers/text boxes as a common parsing issue, and career centers warn ATS may not read header/footer content.
contact & header.notes: International: if your name has diacritics, keep formatting plain to reduce parser risk. CS: include GitHub/portfolio. Biology: include LinkedIn or ORCID only if you actively use it.

contact & header.action: Write links as visible URLs (avoid hiding links behind hyperlinked words/icons).
contact & header.description: Use a plain-text, fully visible LinkedIn profile URL and/or GitHub/portfolio URL.
contact & header.rationale: UMD notes ATS may only display the URL for hyperlinked text (not the linked word), and Utah recommends listing a LinkedIn URL in the contact area.
contact & header.notes: International: ensure URLs are ASCII-friendly. CS: GitHub link is often high-signal. Biology: consider Google Scholar link only if publications exist.

contact & header.action: Replace decorative symbols and uncommon characters with standard text and bullets.
contact & header.description: Remove icons and emoji; use a simple bullet (•) or hyphen (-) and standard punctuation.
contact & header.rationale: Greenhouse notes some resumes import incorrectly due to characters not recognized by its text parsing technology; career-center ATS guidance similarly recommends simple symbols and layouts.
contact & header.notes: International: this is especially important if your document includes bilingual punctuation or non-Latin bullet glyphs. CS/Bio: list tools as words (e.g., Python, PCR) rather than icons.

contact & header.action: Standardize your name (and optional preferred name) to match application records.
contact & header.description: Use the name that matches your legal documents and application records; optionally add preferred/English name in parentheses and phonetic help if desired.
contact & header.rationale: OSU’s international student résumé sample advises listing your name as it appears on legal documents and optionally including a preferred/English name in parentheses, improving consistency across U.S. recruiting processes.
contact & header.notes: International: helps reduce mismatches in ATS profiles. CS/Bio: none beyond consistency.

education.action: Spell out degree names and majors in full at least once.
education.description: Example: “Bachelor of Science in Biology” (not only “B.Sc. Biology”).
education.rationale: UCI recommends spelling out degrees and majors for ATS compatibility; Utah also recommends using both acronyms and full phrases (e.g., “Bachelor of Science” and “B.Sc.”) to improve parsing/recognition.
education.notes: International: reduces ambiguity for foreign credential abbreviations. CS/Bio: include official major name used by your institution.

education.action: List education in reverse chronological order with the degree in progress first.
education.description: Start with your current U.S. institution/degree, then prior degrees (including foreign institutions).
education.rationale: Utah recommends reverse chronological order for ATS compatibility, and OSU’s international sample highlights that U.S. résumés list Education in reverse chronological order starting with the degree in progress.
education.notes: International: helps recruiters quickly anchor your U.S. program. CS/Bio: include expected grad date (Month Year).

education.action: For foreign degrees, add a short equivalency/context note (and convert GPA if included).
education.description: Add a brief parenthetical with U.S.-readable context; if you list a GPA from abroad, convert it to the U.S. 4.0 scale.
education.rationale: OSU’s international student résumé sample recommends providing a frame of reference for foreign institutions and converting GPA values to the U.S. 4.0 scale when included.
education.notes: International: high value when recruiting is U.S.-centric. CS/Bio: ensure course/degree names match U.S. equivalents when possible.

experience.action: Use bullet points (not paragraphs) to describe achievements under each role.
experience.description: Aim for 2–5 bullets per role, each describing an action + outcome.
experience.rationale: Deloitte explicitly recommends bullets instead of paragraphs and a clean format; Utah also recommends bullet points for readability by ATS and humans.
experience.notes: CS: bullets should include scale, latency, throughput, correctness, security. Biology: include assays, sample counts, and outcomes (yield, accuracy).

experience.action: Keep the layout consistent so ATS reading order stays left-to-right, top-to-bottom.
experience.description: Avoid right-justified date blocks that ‘float’; keep titles/companies/dates in predictable text positions.
experience.rationale: UMD notes ATS reads left to right, top to bottom; layouts that don't follow this can scramble what the system captures.
experience.notes: International: avoid side-by-side bilingual experience descriptions. CS/Bio: keep dates and locations in plain text (no tables).

experience.action: Quantify achievements where possible (numbers, %, $, time saved).
experience.description: Add measurable outcomes to at least your top bullets per role.
experience.rationale: Amazon advises using measurable data and quantifying success; Utah and Deloitte also encourage measurable achievements/quantifying outcomes.
experience.notes: CS: quantify performance, reliability, scale, user impact. Biology: quantify sample sizes, throughput, assay sensitivity/specificity, time saved.

experience.action: Spell out job titles (avoid abbreviation-only titles like 'Sr.').
experience.description: Use “Senior Research Assistant” rather than only “Sr. RA” in the title line.
experience.rationale: UCI notes ATS keyword matching depends on exact terms; Greenhouse lists incomplete job titles (e.g., 'Sr.' instead of 'Senior') as a parsing issue that can require manual correction.
experience.notes: International: if translating a title, use a standard U.S. equivalent. CS/Bio: expand role abbreviations (TA, RA) once if unclear.

experience.action: Use official, recognizable employer names (no placeholders; include suffixes when part of the name).
experience.description: Avoid anonymized filler; write the real employer name when possible.
experience.rationale: Greenhouse notes that fake resumes/placeholder data can cause parsing to skip names, and missing identifying words (Inc., Co., LTD, LLC) can contribute to parsing issues.
experience.notes: International: if confidentiality is required, use a real descriptor, but expect weaker parsing/search. CS/Bio: none.

projects.action: Use a clearly labeled Projects section formatted like Experience.
projects.description: Each project: project name + role (if any) + dates + 1–3 bullets; no screenshots.
projects.rationale: Utah recommends using clear headings like Projects for ATS recognition, and Greenhouse notes unclear sections and inconsistent formats can lead to partial parsing.
projects.notes: CS: projects often substitute for internships—prioritize. Biology: include research projects, lab rotations, or data analysis projects.

projects.action: Embed key tools/techniques as plain-text keywords in project bullets.
projects.description: Example keywords: programming languages/frameworks; or lab techniques/assays and analysis tools.
projects.rationale: UCI explains ATS scan résumés for keywords/skills and rank candidates; USC notes ATS-reviewed applications scan for keywords/skills/competencies, so key tools should appear in text.
projects.notes: International: prefer U.S.-standard naming. CS+Bio: include both computational and wet-lab keywords if relevant.

projects.action: Provide portfolio/code links as visible URLs, not embedded images or shapes.
projects.description: Write any project code/portfolio links as fully visible URLs (or DOIs) in the bullet or project line (no embedded shapes).
projects.rationale: UMD warns ATS may mishandle hyperlinked words; Greenhouse notes embedded images/layouts can cause information to upload incorrectly during parsing.
projects.notes: CS: link to best 1–2 repos. Biology: link to preprints/posters only if they add credibility.

skills/tech.action: Create a dedicated Skills section listing hard skills in plain text (no charts).
skills/tech.description: A simple list (bullets or commas) of tools, languages, techniques, and platforms.
skills/tech.rationale: UCI recommends focusing on hard skills for ATS keyword scans; Utah and UMD caution that complex formatting/graphics can hinder parsing.
skills/tech.notes: CS: include Languages + Frameworks + Tools. Biology: include wet-lab + analysis software. CS+Bio: split into 'Programming' and 'Lab/Research'.

skills/tech.description: Examples: Languages, Tools, Methods/Techniques, Cloud/DevOps, Lab Techniques.
skills/tech.rationale: UCI advises using standard section titles; UMD recommends common headings so ATS can sort information correctly.
skills/tech.notes: International: avoid creative labels. CS/Bio: keep category names conventional.

skills/tech.action: Include both acronyms and full forms for key skills/credentials at least once.
skills/tech.rationale: UCI and Utah explicitly recommend using both full phrases and acronyms to help ATS recognize keywords.
skills/tech.notes: CS: use for frameworks/standards (REST, CI/CD). Biology: use for techniques (qPCR—quantitative PCR).

skills/tech.action: Avoid keyword stuffing; show skills in context in bullets.
skills/tech.description: If you list a tool in Skills, also show where you used it (Experience/Projects).
skills/tech.rationale: UCI warns against keyword stuffing and recommends writing naturally while highlighting hard skills.
skills/tech.notes: CS: demonstrate tools in shipped/working projects. Biology: demonstrate techniques with experiment outcomes.

research/publications.action: If applicable, add a plain-text Publications/Presentations section with standard citations.
research/publications.description: Authors + Title + Venue/Journal + Year (and DOI/URL as text if useful).
research/publications.rationale: UCI and UMD advise avoiding graphics and using standard headings; plain-text publications are more reliably parsed and searchable than images or complex formatting.
research/publications.notes: Biology/CS+Bio: publications and posters can be high-signal; keep citations concise. CS: include relevant conference papers if any.

research/publications.action: List research methods and instruments as text keywords (not only in course titles).
research/publications.description: Example: lab techniques, instruments, and analysis software as text in Skills or Research Skills.
research/publications.rationale: Utah notes ATS may check for skills/keywords and recommends prioritizing relevant information; UCI notes ATS scan for skills/qualifications to help recruiters compare candidates.
research/publications.notes: Biology: include lab techniques and compliance training if relevant. CS+Bio: include bioinformatics tools if used.

international-work-authorization.action: Do not list immigration/visa status on your résumé by default.
international-work-authorization.description: Do not add immigration-status lines unless you have a very specific strategy and your advisers recommend it for the situation.
international-work-authorization.rationale: Texas Tech and Georgetown advise that you generally do not need to disclose immigration status on the résumé, while emphasizing you must never lie about your status.
international-work-authorization.notes: International: if you are a U.S. citizen/PR and your name causes confusion, some offices suggest clarifying; discuss with your school adviser.

international-work-authorization.action: If you include a work-authorization line, keep it short and clear.
international-work-authorization.description: One short sentence near contact info or summary (avoid long explanations).
international-work-authorization.rationale: OSU’s international résumé sample states work-authorization status on the résumé is completely optional and provides short example phrasing.
international-work-authorization.notes: International: reserve details for interviews. CS/Bio: won’t override portal eligibility questions; still answer those accurately.

international-work-authorization.action: Answer work-authorization and sponsorship questions truthfully and consistently in every portal.
international-work-authorization.description: Keep answers consistent across all applications; confirm your situation with your DSO if unsure.
international-work-authorization.rationale: NACE explains employers can lawfully ask whether you’re authorized to work and whether you will need future sponsorship; it advises most F-1 students eligible for CPT/OPT can answer 'yes' to authorization and 'yes' to needing sponsorship in almost all cases.
international-work-authorization.notes: International: inconsistencies between résumé statements and portal answers can trigger screening issues. Consult your DSO/career office for edge cases.

international-work-authorization.action: Treat qualifying questions as potential knockout filters and review them before submitting.
international-work-authorization.description: Slow down on questions about experience years, location/work eligibility, and sponsorship—these can auto-filter.
international-work-authorization.rationale: The University of Utah notes applications can be filtered out based on qualifying questions, even when a human will still see the résumé attachment.
international-work-authorization.notes: International: sponsorship questions are often the critical filter. CS/Bio: experience-year questions can be tricky; interpret carefully and answer honestly.

international-work-authorization.action: Keep résumé claims consistent with OPT rules: work must be directly related to your major.
international-work-authorization.description: Avoid claiming OPT-based eligibility for roles unrelated to your major; consult your DSO for grey areas.
international-work-authorization.rationale: OSU’s Office of International Affairs defines OPT as practical training directly related to the major and cautions that OPT employment not directly related to the major is a status violation.
international-work-authorization.notes: CS+Bio: when applying to bioinformatics roles, keep your narrative aligned to the major that will support the work authorization.

formatting & file.action: Use a simple, text-forward layout (avoid design-heavy templates).
formatting & file.description: Single-column, black-and-white, minimal styling; no decorative borders.
formatting & file.rationale: UCI advises avoiding complex layouts and design tools because ATS struggle with images/tables/unusual fonts; Amazon likewise recommends keeping the résumé text-forward, black and white, and simple.
formatting & file.notes: International: avoid non-English decorative fonts. CS/Bio: prioritize content over design.

formatting & file.action: Remove tables, columns, and text boxes (including for dates/skills).
formatting & file.description: If you used a two-column template, convert to one column; replace tables with consistent plain text formatting.
formatting & file.rationale: UVA and UMD warn that tables/columns can prevent ATS from reading your résumé properly; Greenhouse lists tables, column layouts, and text boxes as common causes of parsing errors.
formatting & file.notes: International: bilingual side-by-side columns are high risk. CS: avoid icon-based skill matrices. Biology: avoid lab-skill tables.

formatting & file.action: Avoid images, graphics, photos, and word art.
formatting & file.description: No headshots, logos, charts, or icons used as content.
formatting & file.rationale: UMD notes ATS cannot pull information from images/graphics; Greenhouse lists graphics/photos/word art and image uploads as common parsing issues.
formatting & file.notes: International: photos are common in some countries but discouraged in U.S. résumés and add parsing risk.

formatting & file.action: Use standard fonts and readable font sizes (typically 10–12 pt).
formatting & file.description: Examples: Arial, Calibri, Times New Roman; avoid downloaded/novelty fonts.
formatting & file.rationale: UVA recommends universal fonts in 10–12pt, and UMD warns downloaded fonts can be problematic for ATS.
formatting & file.notes: International: standard fonts improve compatibility across devices and systems.

formatting & file.action: Keep alignment and margins conventional (left-align; ~0.5–1 inch margins).
formatting & file.description: Avoid fully justified body text and extreme margin compression.
formatting & file.rationale: UVA recommends 0.5–1 inch margins and left alignment for readability and ATS compatibility.
formatting & file.notes: CS/Bio: left alignment improves scanability when you have many technical terms.

formatting & file.action: Upload in a standard file type supported by the portal (and follow instructions).
formatting & file.description: When allowed, .docx is often safest; otherwise use a text-based PDF.
formatting & file.rationale: UMD notes file type matters and ATS often prefer .docx; Greenhouse, Workday, and Lever document support for common formats such as .doc/.docx/.pdf/.txt.
formatting & file.notes: International: if your résumé contains multilingual text, test parsing carefully; some ATS have limited non-English parsing.

formatting & file.action: If you submit a PDF, verify it's text-based (not an image scan).
formatting & file.description: You should be able to highlight text in the PDF; if not, export again from Word/Docs.
formatting & file.rationale: Workday notes image-based files are not supported for resume-to-profile uploads; Utah and Greenhouse note that image-based resumes and graphical content can cause ATS to misread or fail to parse.
formatting & file.notes: International: scanning a paper résumé to PDF is especially risky; use digital export.

formatting & file.action: Keep the file size small enough for parsers (avoid heavy media).
formatting & file.description: Remove embedded images and unnecessary media; keep the document text-first.
formatting & file.rationale: Greenhouse states it cannot parse resumes larger than 2.5MB (often because they include high-quality images), a common source of parse failures.
formatting & file.notes: International: large PDFs from design tools are a frequent source of failures. CS/Bio: keep output text-only.

keywords & tailoring.action: Mirror exact job-description keywords and terminology in your résumé content.
keywords & tailoring.description: Use the same phrasing for tools/skills and role requirements (e.g., matching the posting’s wording).
keywords & tailoring.rationale: UCI warns that keyword matching often depends on exact job-description terms; Amazon and Deloitte also recommend aligning accomplishments and keywords with the role’s requirements.
keywords & tailoring.notes: International: translate foreign role terms into common U.S. equivalents. CS: include stack terms used in posting. Biology: include technique and compliance terms used in posting.

keywords & tailoring.action: Include both acronyms and spelled-out forms at least once for key terms.
keywords & tailoring.rationale: UCI explicitly recommends including full terms and acronyms; Utah echoes using both acronyms and full phrases to support recognition.
keywords & tailoring.notes: International: helps when you learned/used alternate naming conventions abroad.

keywords & tailoring.action: Use full, standardized job titles and labels (avoid text-only abbreviations).
keywords & tailoring.description: Example: “Research Assistant (RA)” once, then “Research Assistant” afterward.
keywords & tailoring.rationale: Greenhouse notes incomplete job titles/abbreviations can contribute to parsing issues; UCI emphasizes matching exact terms.
keywords & tailoring.notes: CS: spell out SWE/TPM once. Biology: spell out technique abbreviations once.

keywords & tailoring.action: Avoid keyword stuffing; prioritize relevant hard skills and write naturally.
keywords & tailoring.description: Do not paste blocks of keywords; integrate them into bullets and a structured Skills section.
keywords & tailoring.rationale: UCI explicitly advises focusing on hard skills while avoiding keyword stuffing and maintaining professional tone.
keywords & tailoring.notes: All majors: ATS and recruiters respond better to evidence of skill use than keyword lists alone.

proofreading & QA.action: Proofread for spelling and grammar errors.
proofreading & QA.description: Run spellcheck; read aloud; get a second reviewer.
proofreading & QA.rationale: Utah explicitly notes ATS can check spelling and grammar, and recommends thorough proofreading because errors can reduce professionalism and cause parsing issues.
proofreading & QA.notes: International: if English is not your first language, extra proofreading is high ROI—ask a fluent reviewer.

proofreading & QA.action: Enforce consistency in tense, punctuation, spacing, and style.
proofreading & QA.description: Example: consistent date format; same bullet punctuation across roles.
proofreading & QA.rationale: Utah recommends ensuring consistent formatting and consistency in tense, punctuation, and style as part of ATS-friendly final steps.
proofreading & QA.notes: CS/Bio: consistency reduces the chance that parsers mis-associate dates/roles.

proofreading & QA.action: Remove parser-hostile text patterns (letter-spacing; inconsistent sections).
proofreading & QA.description: Avoid ‘C O M P A N Y’ spaced-out words and ensure each section uses the same pattern.
proofreading & QA.rationale: Greenhouse lists ‘spaces between letters’ and resumes without clear sections/differing formats as common reasons parsing misinterprets content.
proofreading & QA.notes: International: some PDF exports inadvertently add spacing; verify by copying text into a plain text editor.

ATS testing & tracking.action: After uploading, verify that the parsed fields are correct and fix errors before submitting.
ATS testing & tracking.description: If the portal shows an auto-filled profile/application, scan for misplaced dates, missing employers, and blank contact fields.
ATS testing & tracking.rationale: Workday’s guide notes it will populate data from your résumé into your profile and you should review for accuracy; Greenhouse similarly instructs users to verify parsed information because embedded images/layouts can upload incorrectly.
ATS testing & tracking.notes: International: double-check that your name order and phone format were captured. CS/Bio: confirm skills didn’t end up in the wrong field.

ATS testing & tracking.action: Complete every application field—don’t rely on résumé upload alone.
ATS testing & tracking.description: Re-enter experience and education details when asked, even if it feels redundant.
ATS testing & tracking.rationale: UVA advises completing every field because ATS use the application portion to scan your information as well.
ATS testing & tracking.notes: International: portal work-authorization fields usually matter more than résumé wording. CS/Bio: ensure job titles/dates in the form match the résumé.

ATS testing & tracking.action: Maintain a master résumé and a version history to support fast, consistent tailoring.
ATS testing & tracking.description: Keep one ‘master’ file; copy each tailored version into a running document with dates/targets.
ATS testing & tracking.rationale: Utah recommends keeping a master file and saving versions as you tailor for different positions.
ATS testing & tracking.notes: CS: keep multiple skill-stack variants (backend, data, security). Biology: keep variants for research vs. industry lab vs. clinical.

ATS testing & tracking.action: Use a consistent, identifying file name for each version you submit.
ATS testing & tracking.description: Name files with your name + résumé + year + target (role/company).
ATS testing & tracking.rationale: Utah recommends naming the file with your first and last name and notes file naming helps if a résumé detaches; it also suggests a file-naming system when you have multiple versions.
ATS testing & tracking.notes: International: helps recruiters find you even if the ATS shows incomplete parsing.

ATS testing & tracking.action: Run a final automated scan/optimizer and validate the suggestions.
ATS testing & tracking.description: Use campus tools (e.g., USC VMock, UCI Big Resume) or similar; check suggestions for accuracy and keep résumé human-readable.
ATS testing & tracking.rationale: UCI recommends testing match percentage with Big Interview tools; UCI’s Big Resume can compare your résumé to a job posting; USC describes its resume optimizer as preparation for ATS systems that scan for keywords/skills/competencies.
ATS testing & tracking.notes: International: make sure the tool doesn't prompt you to add sensitive personal data. CS/Bio: ensure tool suggestions don't remove technical specificity.

assumptions: No specific target industry, role, GPA threshold, or geographic constraints were provided; guidance assumes a general U.S. internship/entry-level search.
This guidance focuses on ATS parsing/searchability and common U.S. recruiting portals; individual employers may configure systems differently.
International student notes assume typical F-1 pathways (CPT/OPT) but do not constitute legal advice; consult your DSO/international office for individualized guidance.

Because some vendor help pages were partially inaccessible via the browsing tool, some Lever/Workday details rely on publicly crawled help snippets and employer-published Workday guidance, not private Workday documentation.
